title: TPU v1 · 256×256 Systolic-Array Matrix Multiply Unit (MXU)
oneLine: TPU is a real data-center inference accelerator whose compute core is a large systolic array, engineered to execute inference-dominant matrix multiplication at exceptional energy efficiency.

legend.name: Unified Buffer
legend.desc: A 24 MiB software-managed on-chip SRAM scratchpad for activations and intermediate results. TPU removes hardware caches in favor of predictable latency.

legend.name: Weight FIFO
legend.desc: Weights arrive from 8 GiB of off-chip DDR3 through the Weight FIFO and remain fixed in the array after loading—a weight-stationary dataflow.

legend.name: PE array (MXU)
legend.desc: A 256×256 grid of 65,536 8-bit multiply-accumulate units. Data passes directly between PEs with almost no memory access, the foundation of its energy efficiency.

legend.name: Accumulators
legend.desc: A 4 MiB bank of 32-bit accumulators captures partial sums flowing down each column. Low-precision multiplication and wide accumulation control overflow.

legend.name: Activation / normalization
legend.desc: Apply activation and normalization to accumulated results, then write them to the Unified Buffer for the next layer.

steps.cap: ① Preload weights from DDR3 through the Weight FIFO; once loaded, they remain stationary in the array.
② Stream activations from the Unified Buffer across array rows, advancing one PE per cycle.
③ Each PE performs one 8-bit multiply-accumulate and forwards the partial sum to the next PE without accessing memory.
④ Partial sums accumulate down each column and land in 32-bit accumulators.
⑤ Apply activation and normalization, then write results to the Unified Buffer for the next layer.

before: Define the compute-core question: Should the chip use general-purpose CPU cores, a vector engine, or a specialized array? TPU answers with a purpose-built matrix-multiplication machine for inference.
Decisions to make: Target TOPS, numerical precision (INT8, FP16, and so on), dataflow (weight-, output-, or row-stationary), PE-array size, target frequency, area, and power.

Required inputs: Workload characteristics from §3.1—matrix-multiply share, arithmetic intensity, and working-set size—plus sustainable data-supply bandwidth and process-library density, speed, and power.
Hard constraints: The power ceiling limits MAC count; on-chip and off-chip bandwidth limit array utilization; and the software ecosystem must support the chosen numerical format.
during: Commit to the workload: MLP, CNN, and LSTM inference can all be lowered to GEMM. With abundant matrix multiplication, build the chip as a dedicated matrix engine.

Select the structure: A systolic array reuses each value many times after entry and passes data directly between PEs with almost no memory access. That maximizes MACs per area and per watt; the TPU paper emphasizes memory-free transfers among 64K ALUs.
Select the dataflow: In a weight-stationary flow, load weights once, stream activations across rows, and accumulate partial sums downward to maximize weight reuse.

Select precision: Inference tolerates low precision, so use 8-bit integer multiplication with 32-bit accumulation. Compared with floating point, this reduces storage by roughly 4× and makes each MAC smaller and more efficient.
Size the array: The 256×256 choice is bounded jointly by throughput, feed bandwidth, area, and power. A larger array would be underfed when bandwidth cannot scale with it.
Remove overhead: Eliminate caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order execution so silicon goes to MACs, trading generality for deterministic latency and high compute density.
after: Validate performance: Use roofline and cycle-level models on real workloads to measure utilization and determine whether compute or bandwidth dominates. Adjust array size, dataflow, or SRAM if targets are missed.
Propagate requirements: Specify SRAM capacity and bandwidth for memory hierarchy (§3.4), bisection bandwidth for on-chip interconnect (§3.5), and the delivery cadence for dataflow (§3.6).
Hand off to microarchitecture (④): Define PE pipelining and array fill/drain behavior—the subject of ④ §3.1.
Define verification and test hooks: Plan array verification, SRAM MBIST, and performance counters for utilization and stalls.
Preserve margin and configuration: Decide whether precision or array dimensions can be scaled to derive high-, mid-, and low-tier products.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
